opp.ais_why:
• Per the 11 May 2026 rep note: 'Delayed a few weeks due to additional requirements being considered, mainly on ERP integration. They are sending questions this week and we will schedule an integration session.' On 13 Apr 2026: 'Working through RFP and we have some gaps in obligation tracking but they still want Zycus to respond and to see demo of what we can do now and understand what we will commit to adding, within 12 months.' Buyer concerns are ERP integration and obligation tracking ‚Äî pure traditional CLM.
• On 26 Feb 2026: 'Running another demo on their use case, high value energy contracts.' The deal is anchored in high-value energy contracts (CLM use case) ‚Äî not AI capability. Merlin and Merlin Intake appear in product scope but never in dated rep dialogue, RFP commentary, or demo focus across the 2,380-char NextStep.
• Confidence is Medium because the NextStep is moderately rich but covers a discrete CLM evaluation rather than a broad S2P. The absence of any AI keyword (AI, Merlin, agentic, GenAI) across the entire history despite Merlin/Merlin Intake bundling in Products suggests Zycus has positioned AI optionally; buyer is not pulling for it.

The Next Step field describes the operational problem (high-value energy contract management, obligation tracking gaps, ERP integration needs) but never quantifies the cost of the current state: FTE hours spent manually tracking obligations, cost of missed obligations or breaches, cycle time to execute contracts, or expected ROI from the relevant stakeholder 10 Feb the Next Step mentions 'small opportunity' and '1 higher priority project in motion', signaling that without financial justification this $110k procurement may be deprioritized. On 13 Apr the buyer acknowledged gaps in Zycus obligation tracking but still wanted a demo and roadmap commitment, suggesting their pain is real but not urgent enough to override the competing priority. The absence of a quantified value case means the Economic Buyer (Nick Middleton, Deputy General Counsel) and any CFO-level approver lack the financial anchor to prioritize this procurement over other initiatives. The decision has stalled three times in seven weeks with no urgency injection, consistent with an unquantified business case. Recommended move: run a value-quantification workshop with Nick Middleton and the contract management team to model the cost of manual obligation tracking (hours, risk exposure, breach penalties) and the ROI of automation, then anchor the decision to that financial case.

competition.narrative: Ironclad is the confirmed live competitor in a two-vendor shortlist RFP. On 25 Mar the Next Step states 'PM has been told to issue RFP to both shortlisted vendors (he was trying to avoid this)', indicating the procurement manager preferred a sole-source decision (likely for Zycus, given the 10 Feb 'Zycus well preferred' note) but was overruled and forced to run a competitive RFP between Zycus and Ironclad. On 16 Mar the Next Step states 'preference is for Zycus and have offered LOI in Mar but we are pushing very hard for contract signing and close', confirming Zycus was preferred after initial commercials and demos, and the buyer offered a Letter of Intent. However, 108 days later (as of 3 Jul) no contract has been signed, the deal remains in Shortlisted stage, and the buyer has been disengaged for 39 days (last verified touch 25 May). This pattern suggests either Ironclad has closed the gap (possibly stronger on obligation tracking or ERP integration, the two areas where Zycus has acknowledged gaps and delays), or the buyer is using the extended evaluation to pressure both vendors on pricing and roadmap commitments. On 13 Apr the Next Step acknowledges 'we have some gaps in obligation tracking but they still want Zycus to respond and to see demo of what we can do now and understand what we will commit to adding, within 12 months', indicating Zycus is competing on a future-capability bet (roadmap commitment) rather than current product strength in obligation tracking, which is a vulnerability if Ironclad has a stronger current-state product. The competitive wedge for Zycus is likely Merlin (AI-driven intake, negotiation agent, contract intelligence) applied to high-value energy contracts, but there is no evidence in the Next Step or Tasks that the Merlin/AI narrative has been activated in this deal. The Products field includes 'Merlin;Merlin Intake', indicating Merlin is in scope, but the Next Step describes only CLM use-cases (demos, obligation tracking, ERP integration) with no mention of AI positioning. If Ironclad is positioning a simpler, faster-to-implement CLM solution without the AI premium, Zycus may be over-scoped and over-priced for a 'small opportunity' (per 10 Feb Next Step) that is already deprioritized internally. Recommended move: Run a competitive review call with Luke Davies within 7 days to understand Ironclad's positioning (pricing, obligation tracking depth, ERP integration story, timeline), confirm where Zycus stands, and activate the Merlin/AI wedge if it is relevant to SFV's energy contract intelligence needs. If Merlin is not a buyer priority, consider descoping to simplify and de-risk the close.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is confirmed and specific: Scheme Financial Vehicle manages high-value, complex energy contracts with significant obligation tracking requirements, and their current manual or legacy system cannot handle the depth of obligation tracking, ERP integration, and NSW government systems integration they need. On 26 Feb the Next Step describes 'their use case, high value energy contracts', confirming the domain is energy procurement with high financial and compliance stakes. On 13 Apr the Next Step states 'we have some gaps in obligation tracking but they still want Zycus to respond and to see demo of what we can do now and understand what we will commit to adding, within 12 months', indicating the buyer has explicitly identified obligation tracking gaps in their current process and is evaluating whether Zycus (despite acknowledged gaps in the product) can meet their needs via roadmap commitment. On 11 May the Next Step adds 'Delayed a few weeks due to additional requirements being considered, mainly on ERP integration', confirming that integration with their ERP and NSW government systems is a critical pain point blocking the decision. On 4 Mar the Next Step mentions 'responses to security requests', indicating that data safety and InfoSec for a government-linked entity is a concern. On 10 Feb the Next Step states 'small opportunity with Zycus well preferred- waiting on internal green light- 1 higher priority project in motion', suggesting that while the pain is real, it is not urgent enough to override a competing internal priority, which is consistent with the lack of quantified business case. The pain owner is likely Nick Middleton (Deputy General Counsel & Head of Contract Management) and Chitra Shende (Contract Manager), who would be accountable for contract compliance, obligation tracking, and breach risk. However, neither has been visibly engaged in driving urgency, and the pain has not been quantified (no cost of breaches, FTE hours, or ROI stated), which explains the three decision delays and 106 days in stage. Recommended move: Run a pain-quantification session with Nick Middleton and Chitra Shende to model the cost of the current manual obligation tracking process (hours per contract, risk of missed obligations, historical breach costs, compliance exposure) and anchor the decision to that financial and compliance risk.

paper_process.narrative: The contracting and legal process is entirely opaque. On 16 Mar the Next Step states 'preference is for Zycus and have offered LOI in Mar but we are pushing very hard for contract signing and close', indicating the buyer offered a Letter of Intent and Zycus expected to move to contracting in Mar. 108 days later (as of 3 Jul) the deal remains in Shortlisted stage with no MSA, SOW, or contract negotiation visible. On 4 Mar the Next Step mentions 'responses to security requests', confirming InfoSec review is required (multi-tenant SaaS for a government-linked financial vehicle), but there is no record of InfoSec approval, data safety sign-off, or any subsequent security milestone. On 11 May the Next Step states Carl (the deal owner's manager) is 'on standby to F2F visit when needed', suggesting a face-to-face contract close was anticipated, but no visit is recorded. The Next Step provides no visibility into legal review, MSA/SOW templates, redlining, procurement channel (direct vs reseller/partner), or whether implementation scoping is formalized. The absence of any contracting motion despite a 16 Mar LOI offer and a stated preference for Zycus indicates either the paper process has not been initiated (the deal is stuck in vendor selection / requirements refinement and has not progressed to commercial), or the buyer is slow-walking the contract while they evaluate Ironclad. For a government-linked entity, a formal procurement process with legal review, CFO sign-off, and possibly board or audit-committee approval is likely required, but none of this is documented. The 31 Aug close date (59 days out) is not credible without immediate contract kickoff. Recommended move: In the next call with the buyer (Luke Davies and Nick Middleton), explicitly map the paper process: who drafts the contract, what is the legal review cycle, what approvals are required (General Counsel, CFO, board), and what is the realistic timeline from vendor selection to signature. If the LOI from 16 Mar is still valid, activate it and push to the relevant stakeholder stage with a contract negotiation kickoff within 14 days.

On 13 Apr 2026: 'Working through RFP and we have some gaps in obligation tracking but they still want Zycus to respond and to see demo of what we can do now and understand what we will commit to adding, within 12 months.' • On 26 Feb 2026: 'Running another demo on their use case, high value energy contracts.' The deal is anchored in high-value energy contracts (CLM use case) ‚Äî not AI capability. Merlin and Merlin Intake appear in product scope but never in dated rep dialogue, RFP commentary, or demo focus across the 2,380-char NextStep.
• Confidence is Medium because the NextStep is moderately rich but covers a discrete CLM evaluation rather than a broad S2P. The absence of any AI keyword (AI, Merlin, agentic, GenAI) across the entire history despite Merlin/Merlin Intake bundling in Products suggests Zycus has positioned AI optionally; buyer is not pulling for it.
